quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 11 Quiz 10
quiz.subtitle: Using Volume in Real-World Problems Like Mass and Density
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: What is the formula for density?
options: $D = \frac{m}{V}$ where $m$ is mass and $V$ is volume
questions.explanation: Density is mass per unit volume: $D = \frac{m}{V}$. It is typically measured in g/cm$^3$, kg/m$^3$, or lb/ft$^3$. Rearranging: $m = D \times V$ and $V = \frac{m}{D}$. Density describes how tightly packed matter is in a substance.

questions.question: A block of aluminum has volume $40$ cm$^3$. Aluminum has density $2.7$ g/cm$^3$. What is the mass of the block?
questions.explanation: $m = D \times V = 2.7 \times 40 = 108$ grams. Mass equals density multiplied by volume. This is a direct application of the density formula.

questions.question: A piece of metal has mass $450$ g and volume $60$ cm$^3$. What is its density?
questions.explanation: $D = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{450}{60} = 7.5$ g/cm$^3$. This is close to the density of iron or steel. Water has density $1$ g/cm$^3$, so this metal is $7.5$ times denser than water.

questions.question: A gold statue has mass $19{,}300$ grams. Gold has density $19.3$ g/cm$^3$. What is the statue's volume?
questions.explanation: $V = \frac{m}{D} = \frac{19{,}300}{19.3} = 1{,}000$ cm$^3$. This is a cube about $10$ cm on each side. Gold is very dense â€” a surprisingly small volume can have a large mass.

questions.question: A cylindrical iron rod has radius $2$ cm and length $50$ cm. Iron has density $7.8$ g/cm$^3$. What is the rod's mass, rounded to the nearest gram?
questions.explanation: $V = \pi r^2 h = \pi(4)(50) = 200\pi \approx 628.32$ cm$^3$. $m = D \times V = 7.8 \times 628.32 \approx 4{,}900.9 \approx 4{,}901$ grams (about $4.9$ kg). First find volume, then multiply by density.

questions.question: A solid sphere has radius $5$ cm and mass $2{,}618$ grams. What is its density, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi(5^3) = \frac{500\pi}{3} \approx 523.6$ cm$^3$. $D = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{2{,}618}{523.6} \approx 5.0$ g/cm$^3$. This is roughly the density of Earth's core â€” mostly iron and nickel.

questions.question: A cylindrical water tank has radius $4$ ft and height $10$ ft. Water has density $62.4$ lb/ft$^3$. What is the mass of water when the tank is full, rounded to the nearest pound?
questions.explanation: $V = \pi(4^2)(10) = 160\pi \approx 502.65$ ft$^3$. $m = 62.4 \times 502.65 \approx 31{,}365.6 \approx 31{,}366$ pounds (about $15.7$ tons). Water is heavy â€” even a modest tank holds significant weight.

questions.question: A cylindrical apartment building has radius $30$ ft and height $80$ ft. It houses $400$ residents. What is the population density in residents per thousand cubic feet, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: $V = \pi(30^2)(80) = 72{,}000\pi \approx 226{,}195$ ft$^3 = 226.195$ thousand ft$^3$. Density $= \frac{400}{226.195} \approx 1.8$ residents per thousand ft$^3$. Population density can use volume just like mass density.

questions.question: An artist carves a sculpture from a rectangular block of marble measuring $3 \times 2 \times 1.5$ ft. Marble costs $\$180$ per cubic foot. What is the cost of the marble?
questions.explanation: $V = 3 \times 2 \times 1.5 = 9$ ft$^3$. Cost $= 9 \times \$180 = \$1{,}620$. Cost based on volume is common in construction and art â€” the larger the block, the more material used and the higher the cost.

questions.question: A shipping container is a rectangular prism measuring $40 \times 8 \times 8.5$ ft. It is completely filled with cargo of average density $35$ lb/ft$^3$. What is the total mass of the cargo, rounded to the nearest hundred pounds?
questions.explanation: $V = 40 \times 8 \times 8.5 = 2{,}720$ ft$^3$. $m = 35 \times 2{,}720 = 95{,}200$ pounds. That's about $47.6$ tons â€” typical for a fully loaded shipping container. Density $\times$ volume gives total mass regardless of how the cargo is arranged.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered density and its applications. You can find mass, volume, or density from the other two variables, apply these to real solids (cylinders, spheres, prisms), and solve cost and population density problems.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) $D = \frac{m}{V}$, $m = DV$, $V = \frac{m}{D}$, (2) First find volume using the appropriate formula, then apply the density equation, (3) Density has units like g/cm$^3$ or lb/ft$^3$, (4) Cost and other quantities can be proportional to volume just like mass is.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) rearranging the density formula correctly depending on what you're solving for, (2) computing volume first â€” you need it for any density problem involving a solid, (3) keeping track of units â€” they should cancel to leave mass units, (4) population density works the same way: amount divided by volume.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Density relates mass and volume: $D = \frac{m}{V}$. To solve problems: (1) Identify what you're given and what you need, (2) If volume isn't given, compute it using $V = Bh$, $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, etc., (3) Use $m = DV$ or $V = \frac{m}{D}$ as needed, (4) For cost or population density, the same principle applies â€” multiply rate by volume or divide total by volume.
